data.prompt:
<execute-bead>
  <bead id="ddx-c7d9054d">
    <title>helix run: review agent rc=1 every cycle — REVIEW_STATUS trailer missing</title>
    <description>
During a 12-cycle 'helix run --agent claude --summary' against the heimq repo on 2026-04-26, every single post-implementation review step returned rc=1 with 0 tokens and the harness logged 'review output missing REVIEW_STATUS trailer'. The harness salvaged each cycle because the tracker showed progress (closed N → N+1), but this means:

1. The structured REVIEW_STATUS verdict is never captured — we pay for the review cycle but lose its decision (block / approve / approve-with-followups).
2. The salvage path masks the fact that reviews are not actually gating anything.
3. Operators reading summary lines see rc=1 'failures' that are actually invisible reviews — confusing signal.

Sample log lines from /Users/erik/Projects/heimq/.helix-logs/helix-20260426-144028.log (run launched 14:40 UTC, completed 16:28 UTC):

  --- ddx agent run [14:51:08] harness=claude rc=0 exit=1 elapsed=350s cost=/bin/zsh tokens=0 ---
  --- end ddx agent output ---
  helix: review output missing REVIEW_STATUS trailer
  helix: review output was not safely interpretable after cycle 1 — continuing

This pattern repeated for every single one of the 12 cycles (same 0-tokens, same 'review output missing REVIEW_STATUS trailer' message after each cycle's review step).

Hypotheses to investigate:
- Review prompt template emitted to Claude does not actually instruct the agent to print a REVIEW_STATUS trailer in a parseable form.
- Prompt does instruct, but the trailer format the harness expects has drifted from what the prompt asks for.
- Claude's output is being truncated before the trailer (timeouts? token caps?).
- Review agent invocation is silently failing very early (0 tokens reported suggests it never got far) — could be a misconfigured CLI invocation, missing flag, env var.

Investigation steps:
1. Inspect what prompt/system message the helix harness sends to claude for the review step — confirm it asks for a REVIEW_STATUS line.
2. Inspect what the harness regexes for to detect the trailer.
3. Run one review step manually with verbose logging to capture full agent stdout.
4. Compare with codex agent — does the trailer issue exist there too, or is it claude-specific?

Why this matters: helix-run's correctness model assumes reviews actually gate progression. Right now reviews are decorative.
    </description>
    <acceptance>
Either: (a) review step succeeds (rc=0, REVIEW_STATUS trailer present, structured verdict captured by harness) on a fresh helix run cycle against any repo with --agent claude, OR (b) the review-trailer requirement is removed from the harness and salvage becomes the explicit primary path with appropriate logging. Whichever decision is made, the operator-facing summary lines should not show rc=1 for an outcome that the harness considers normal.
    </acceptance>
    <labels>helix, area:helix-run, kind:bug, component:review-step</labels>
    <metadata base-rev="5af3b4628a816e32445f7fde3c4f65dac7142f11" bundle=".ddx/executions/20260427T021944-c01742e6"/>
  </bead>
  <governing>
    <note>No governing references were pre-resolved. The bead description above is the primary contract. If it names files, specs, or prior beads, read those before editing. Ground decisions in what is already in the repository; do not guess.</note>
  </governing>
  <instructions>
You are executing one bead inside an isolated DDx execution worktree. The bead's <description> and <acceptance> sections above are the completion contract — every AC checkbox must be provably satisfied by a specific piece of code, test, or file you can point to after your commit.

## How to work

1. Read first. If the bead description names files, read them to see what is already there. If the bead references a spec, ADR, or API contract, read the relevant sections before writing code. Do not start editing until you understand what the bead wants changed and why.

2. Cross-reference your work against the acceptance criteria as you go. Before committing, walk through the AC one item at a time and identify the specific test name, file path, or function that satisfies each checkbox. If you cannot point to concrete evidence for an AC item, it is not done.

3. Run the project's verification commands before committing. Use whatever commands match the crate or package you touched — typically `cargo test` and `cargo clippy` for Rust, `bun test` or `npm test` for JS/TS, `go test` for Go. The bead description usually names the exact commands. **Do not commit red code.** If a test or lint fails, fix it first.

4. Commit once, when everything is green. Stage only the files you intentionally changed with `git add <specific-paths>` — never `git add -A`, because there may be unrelated WIP in this worktree. Use a conventional-commit-style subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. DDx will merge your commits back to the base branch.

5. If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write your reasoning to `.ddx/executions/20260427T021944-c01742e6/no_changes_rationale.txt` with: (a) what is done, (b) what is blocking, (c) what a follow-up attempt would need. Do NOT commit partial, exploratory, or red code hoping the reviewer will accept it — a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit. The bead will be re-queued for another attempt, potentially with a stronger model.

## The bead contract overrides project defaults

The bead description and AC override any CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or project-level conservative defaults in this worktree. If the bead asks for new documentation, write it. If the bead adds a new module or crate, add it. Conservative rules (YAGNI, DOWITYTD, no-docs-unless-asked) do not apply inside execute-bead — the bead IS the ask.

## Quality bar and the review step

After your commit merges, an automated review step may check your work against the acceptance criteria. If any AC item is unmet, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher-capability model, and the review findings are threaded into the next attempt's prompt as a `<review-findings>` section. **The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet the AC in this pass so the bead closes cleanly.**

If this prompt already contains a `<review-findings>` section, address every BLOCKING finding before claiming the work complete. Those findings are the precise list of what the previous attempt missed.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is already initialized. Running it corrupts the bead queue and project configuration.
- Do not modify files outside the scope the bead description names.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or any other project-instructions file unless the bead explicitly asks for it.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and you have verified every AC item, stop. Return control to the orchestrator. Do not continue to explore the repository, run extra tests, or generate follow-up notes — the bead is complete, and returning promptly is how execute-bead signals success.
  </instructions>
</execute-bead>

data.output: No changes made. The bead targets HELIX harness behavior (review-prompt template, REVIEW_STATUS trailer parser, salvage path) — all of which live in the separate `~/Projects/helix` repo, not in this DDx worktree. Verified by grepping: `REVIEW_STATUS` and `review output missing` appear only in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`; no helix-run code exists here. Wrote `no_changes_rationale.txt` with reasoning and suggested re-routing to the helix repo (or re-scoping to a DDx-side interface change like a structured agent-result file, if that's actually what's wanted).
